name: Teresa de Cofrentes and Cinto de la Cabra Loop
name_original: El Alto del Cinto de la Cabra — Circular desde Teresa de Cofrentes
description: Challenge yourself on The High of the Goat's Belt, a difficult 32.9-mile road cycling route from Teresa de Cofrentes with 4220 feet of gain.
region_intro: Road cycling around Teresa De Cofrentes offers a diverse and challenging environment within the Valle de Ayora-Cofrentes in Valencia, Spain. The region is characterized by the Caroig massif, featuring "muelas" (table-top mountains) and "barrancos" (ravines), which create significant elevation changes. Cyclists can expect varied topography with numerous climbs and descents, alongside picturesque river valleys carved by the Júcar River and its tributaries. This landscape provides a robust setting for road cycling routes. Best road cycling routes around Teresa De Cofrentes The most popular road cycling route is Teresa de Cofrentes and Cinto de la Cabra Loop, a 32.9 miles (53.0 km) trail that takes 3 hours 25 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain, making it a demanding ride. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Cofrentes Castle – Brilliant descent to Casas del Rio loop from Ayora, a difficult 81.2 miles (130.6 km) path. This long route includes substantial climbing and offers views of the Júcar River and historic sites. Local road cyclists also love the La Pileta Viewpoint – Alto de Ayora Hut loop from Ayora, a 62.9 miles (101.2 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 6 hours 0 minutes. This route provides demanding climbs and panoramic viewpoints. Road cycling around Teresa De Cofrentes is defined by the Caroig massif, dramatic barrancos, and the Júcar River valleys. The network offers options for moderate to difficult ability levels, with 11 moderate and 31 difficult routes available. The routes in Teresa De Cofrentes are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 3 reviews. More than 800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Teresa De Cofrentes's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Teresa De Cofrentes?
A: There are over 40 road cycling routes available around Teresa De Cofrentes. These routes offer a range of difficulties, with 11 moderate and 31 difficult options, catering to various skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Teresa De Cofrentes?
A: The region is dominated by the Caroig massif, featuring dramatic 'muelas' (table-top mountains) and 'barrancos' (ravines). This means you can expect significant elevation changes, with numerous climbs and descents. Routes often traverse picturesque river valleys carved by the Júcar River and its tributaries, offering a diverse and stimulating environment for cyclists.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Teresa De Cofrentes?
A: The climate in Teresa De Cofrentes is generally favorable for cycling. Spring and autumn offer mild temperatures, making them ideal seasons for exploring the region by bike. While summer can be warm, many routes are still enjoyable, especially during cooler parts of the day.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes in Teresa De Cofrentes?
A: Yes, Teresa De Cofrentes is well-known for its challenging road cycling routes, with 31 routes classified as difficult. Many routes feature substantial elevation gains, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31470075" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cofrentes Castle – Brilliant descent to Casas del Rio loop from Ayora&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 130.6 km with over 2,100 meters of elevation gain. The region's mountainous terrain, including peaks like Pico del Caroig, ensures demanding rides for serious cyclists.

Q: Are there options for less experienced road cyclists in Teresa De Cofrentes?
A: While the region is known for its challenging terrain, there are 11 moderate road cycling routes available. These routes still offer a great way to experience the area's beauty without the extreme demands of the more difficult climbs. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20130643" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ermita de San José – Ayora loop from Ayora&lt;/a&gt;, a 56.1 km route with over 660 meters of elevation gain.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along road cycling routes in Teresa De Cofrentes?
A: You can encounter several natural attractions. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3178996" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Corbinet Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; is a beautiful spot with lush vegetation, perfect for a refreshing stop. You might also come across the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3216823" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arroyo de Cortes Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2746974" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of the Júcar River&lt;/a&gt; as routes often follow its course. The region also features significant peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6276620" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de Caroche&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or points of interest accessible by road bike?
A: Yes, the region offers historical landmarks. &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2751423" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chirel Castle&lt;/a&gt; is an important site offering incredible views of the Muela de Cortes and the Júcar River. The nearby Cerro del Castillo is crowned by the ruins of the Hermitage of Santa Cruz, providing another historical viewpoint. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4600612" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calicanto raft shelter&lt;/a&gt; is a historical site you might encounter.

Q: Where can I find scenic viewpoints on road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer breathtaking views. The summit of Pico del Caroig (1,126 meters) provides panoramic vistas across much of the Valencian region, and on clear days, even the sea is visible. &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2751423" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chirel Castle&lt;/a&gt; and Cerro del Castillo also serve as excellent viewpoints. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20131477" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Pileta Viewpoint – Alto de Ayora Hut loop from Ayora&lt;/a&gt; are specifically named for their scenic outlooks.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Teresa De Cofrentes?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in Teresa De Cofrentes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e839936179/teresa-de-cofrentes-and-cinto-de-la-cabra-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Teresa de Cofrentes and Cinto de la Cabra Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24530718" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente del Jarral – Ayora loop from Ayora&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Teresa De Cofrentes?
A: The road cycling routes in Teresa De Cofrentes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars. Cyclists often praise the diverse and challenging terrain, the stunning natural landscapes, and the quiet roads that wind through the Caroig massif and along the Júcar River. The combination of physical challenge and beautiful scenery is a common highlight.

Q: Are there places to stop for rest or refreshments along the routes?
A: Yes, the region offers recreational areas like Nacimiento and Velilla, which are perfect for rest stops during longer rides. These areas often provide picnic facilities and a chance to enjoy the natural surroundings, sometimes with fresh water springs. While specific cafes or pubs on every route are not guaranteed, these designated spots offer good opportunities to pause and refuel.

Q: Is parking available for road cyclists in Teresa De Cofrentes?
A: While specific parking facilities for cyclists are not explicitly detailed for every trailhead, Teresa De Cofrentes is a municipality that caters to outdoor activities. You can generally find parking in the main towns or designated recreational areas, especially near popular starting points for routes. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.
tips: An incredible site for its views and the arrival road to it. Going up Teresa de Cofrentes or Jalance, the route is amazing, with stretches of demanding slope but the views are more than rewarding. It is a round trip road so on the way back you will have to have your brakes ready ;)

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult and requires very good fitness. It's considered a hard road ride due to significant elevation changes, with over 1,200 meters of climbing.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: On average, cyclists can expect to complete this route in approximately 3 hours and 25 minutes. However, your actual time may vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route is predominantly on mostly well-paved surfaces that are generally easy to ride. However, be aware that some segments, especially on longer variations, may be unpaved and more challenging. The region offers diverse landscapes, including canyons, rolling hills, and mountainous terrain.

faqs.question: What are the scenic highlights along the way?
faqs.answer: One notable highlight on this route is the Alto del Cinto de la Cabra Viewpoint, which offers stunning views. The entire El Valle de Cofrentes-Ayora region is known for its natural beauty, providing picturesque backdrops throughout your ride.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular one starting from Teresa de Cofrentes. While specific parking details aren't provided, Teresa de Cofrentes is a small town, and you can typically find parking in or around the town center to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle in the Valle de Ayora-Cofrentes region?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for road cycling on public roads and trails in the Valle de Ayora-Cofrentes region. However, always respect local regulations and private property.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is not suitable for beginners. It is classified as difficult and requires very good fitness due to its significant elevation gain and challenging nature. Beginners should look for easier routes in the area.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, and while dogs are generally not suitable companions for such a demanding ride, if you are considering bringing a dog, it would need to be in a specialized bike trailer. Always ensure your pet's safety and comfort, and check local regulations regarding pets in public areas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle in the Valencia region, including this route, is typically during the spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) months. During these periods, the weather is usually milder and more pleasant for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with sections of other official trails. You'll find it overlaps with parts of the Comunitat Valenciana. Fredes - el Pinós, Los Castillos del Valle, El Alto de Alcola, Jarafuel, Muela de Juey y el Castillico, and Camino de Bicorp.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to refuel along the route?
faqs.answer: As a circular route starting and ending in Teresa de Cofrentes, you'll find amenities like cafes and restaurants in the town itself. For stops along the route, it's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, as services may be sparse in the more remote sections of the ride.
